body:
The air crackles with a static you can’t quite place. It’s the smell of ozone after a lightning strike, the taste of pennies on your tongue. You’re standing on a street you almost recognize, but the storefronts are hollowed-out eyes staring into a bruised twilight sky. Papers, like skeletal leaves, skitter across the asphalt. In the distance, a siren wails, a lonely, mechanical ghost. Others have walked this exact path and survived it.. You can feel them, the others, a thrum of shared anxiety just beneath the pavement. This isn’t just your dream. It’s our dream.

The Shared Nightmares of a World in Crisis

When the world holds its breath, the collective unconscious exhales into our sleep. A global pandemic that locks down cities, a declaration of war that echoes across continents, a planet groaning under the fever of climate change—these are not distant headlines filtered through a screen. They are intimate invasions. They slip past the sentries of our waking minds, the carefully constructed walls of denial and distraction, and seep into the fertile, chaotic soil of our dreams. There, they sprout into shared landscapes of anxiety and confusion. Suddenly, thousands, even millions, are dreaming of tidal waves that swallow coastlines, of faceless, menacing crowds, of a desperate, frantic search for a lost child in a city of empty streets.

This is not coincidence. It is a psychic resonance, a testament to the profound and often unsettling truth that our individual psyches are not walled-off fortresses. We are porous, permeable beings, afloat in a vast, turbulent sea of shared consciousness. When a shockwave of trauma hits the collective, we all feel the tremor in our sleep. The dreamscape becomes a mirror, reflecting the jagged edges of our collective fear, our unprocessed grief, our desperate, unspoken questions. To dismiss these dreams as mere personal anxiety, a product of too much late-night news, is to miss the point entirely. It’s a form of spiritual bypassing, a comfortable lie we tell ourselves to maintain the illusion of control. It’s a way of pretending we are separate, untouched, immune. You are not immune. You are part of this, and your dreams are screaming this truth at you every night.

May you touch with intention and receive without armor.

Consider the sheer volume of anecdotal evidence. Following major earthquakes, people in the affected regions report dreams of the earth shaking, of buildings crumbling, of being trapped. During the height of the COVID-19 pandemic, dream researchers documented a global surge in anxiety-ridden dreams, with common themes of contagion, confinement, and respiratory distress. These are not isolated incidents. They are a pattern, a chorus of voices from the depths of our shared human experience, all singing the same song of fear and uncertainty. To ignore this chorus is to turn a deaf ear to the wisdom of your own soul.

Your Psyche on High Alert: Why You’re Dreaming of Tsunamis and Empty Streets

Your nervous system is an exquisitely sensitive instrument, calibrated for survival over millennia of evolution. In times of global upheaval, it is humming at a higher frequency, constantly scanning for threats, both real and perceived. This state of hyper-arousal, this low-grade, persistent fight-or-flight response, doesn’t simply switch off when you go to sleep. Instead, it bleeds into your dreams, painting them with the vibrant, terrifying colors of your waking fears. The tsunami that crashes over your city in a dream is not just a random, meaningless symbol; it is the felt sense of being overwhelmed by forces beyond your control, a perfect and visceral metaphor for the deluge of terrifying news and uncertain futures.

Neuroscientist and sleep expert Matthew Walker has written extensively on the functions of sleep and dreaming, explaining how our brains process emotions and consolidate memories during the REM (Rapid Eye Movement) stage of sleep. During times of intense collective stress, our dream-work becomes a frantic, high-stakes attempt to metabolize a shared trauma. The dream of wandering through empty, silent streets, a recurring motif in times of pandemic and social unrest, is a powerful and poignant depiction of social isolation, of the profound disconnection and alienation that can accompany a global crisis. Your brain is not malfunctioning; it is doing exactly what it is designed to do: attempting to make sense of a world that feels senseless, to find a narrative in the chaos, to process the emotional charge of your waking life. It is a desperate, beautiful, and often terrifying process.

In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen that these dreams are not just a replay of waking fears, but a creative and often symbolic processing of them. The dream of being unprepared for an exam, a common anxiety dream, can take on a new and more potent meaning during a global crisis, representing a feeling of being fundamentally unequipped to face the challenges ahead. The dream of your teeth falling out, another classic, can speak to a loss of power, of voice, of the ability to provide for oneself and one’s family. These are not just your personal neuroses; they are the archetypal language of the human soul, speaking to the deepest and most universal of our fears.

I am committed to endless, forensic forgiveness.

I’ve sat with thousands of people navigating exactly this territory. The patterns are remarkably consistent.

The Illusion of Separation: What Collective Dreams Reveal About Our Interconnectedness

We are fed a steady diet of individualism, a myth of the self-made man, the sovereign individual, beholden to no one. But our dreams tell a different, more ancient story. They whisper the truth of our radical interconnectedness. The fact that you and a stranger halfway across the world can have the same dream on the same night—a dream of a falling star, of a talking animal, of a city made of glass—is a crack in the facade of our separateness. It is a glimpse into the underlying unity of consciousness, the shared psychic field in which we are all participants, whether we are aware of it or not.

To acknowledge this interconnectedness is to take on a profound responsibility. It is to understand that your fear is not just your fear; it is a current in the collective ocean. Your healing is not just your healing; it is a contribution to the healing of the whole. This is not a comforting platitude to be cross-stitched on a pillow. It is a fierce and demanding truth. It asks you to look beyond the narrow confines of your own ego, your own personal story of suffering, and to recognize yourself in the dreams of others. It asks you to feel the weight of our shared destiny, the burden and the beauty of our shared humanity. This is the real work of spirituality, not the fluffy, feel-good, ‘love and light’ nonsense that is so often peddled as enlightenment.

This is not a new idea. Mystics and sages from traditions across the globe have spoken of this underlying unity for millennia. From the Hindu concept of Brahman, the ultimate reality that underlies all phenomena, to the Buddhist notion of interconnectedness, to the Christian idea of the mystical body of Christ, the message is the same: we are not separate. We are one. Our dreams are simply reminding us of a truth we have forgotten, a truth that our waking minds, with their endless chatter and their obsession with boundaries and distinctions, can no longer access. To listen to our dreams is to come home to ourselves, and to each other.

If some Gwyneth goo-loving bliss junkie tells you that mercury retrograde will solve everything — encourage them never to breed.

Decoding the Symbols: Common Threads in Global Event Dreams

While the specific details of our dreams are as unique as our own individual lives, the archetypal symbols that emerge during global events are often strikingly similar. Water, in all its forms, is a common and potent motif: tidal waves, floods, murky depths, the feeling of drowning. These dreams speak to the feeling of being emotionally overwhelmed, of being submerged in collective grief and fear. Dreams of being chased or hunted, of being a refugee in your own land, point to a sense of powerlessness, of being at the mercy of forces we cannot control. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen that the dream of losing one’s teeth, a classic anxiety dream, becomes rampant during times of economic instability, a literal representation of losing one’s grip, one’s ability to provide.

To decode these symbols is not to find a simple, one-to-one correspondence in a dream dictionary. That is a fool’s errand. It is to engage in a deep and intimate dialogue with your own psyche. What does the tidal wave feel like in your body? Is it a cleansing force, or a destructive one? What is the specific quality of the fear you experience when you are being chased? Is it a hot, panicked fear, or a cold, existential dread? These are not intellectual questions to be answered from the neck up. They are invitations to a deeper, more embodied understanding of your own experience. The dream is not a puzzle to be solved, but a landscape to be explored, a message from the deepest part of yourself, for the healing of yourself and the world. The American Psychological Association offers resources for understanding the psychological impact of collective trauma, which can be a useful starting point for this exploration. You can find more information on their website at www.apa.org.

Other common symbols include fire, representing both destruction and purification; earthquakes, symbolizing a shaking of the foundations of our lives; and the appearance of wild animals, which can represent the eruption of our own instinctual nature in response to the breakdown of social order. The key is to approach these symbols with a sense of curiosity and reverence, to see them not as random firings of your neurons, but as sacred messengers from the depths of your being.

From Collective Anxiety to Collective Healing: A Path Forward

It is easy to drown in the sea of collective anxiety, to become paralyzed by the sheer scale of the world’s suffering. But our dreams, even the most terrifying ones, are not a curse. They are a call to action. They are a summons to a deeper level of engagement with ourselves and with the world. The path forward is not to numb ourselves with endless scrolling, to distract ourselves with empty consumerism, to pretend that everything is fine. The path forward is to feel, to grieve, to rage, and to allow ourselves to be transformed by the fire of our collective experience.

This is not a passive process of simply witnessing your own pain. It is an active, ongoing practice of turning towards the darkness, both within and without, with courage and compassion. It is the work of building resilience, not as a form of armor, but as a form of flexibility, an ability to bend without breaking. It is the work of cultivating compassion, not as a sentimental feeling, but as a fierce and unwavering commitment to the well-being of all. It is the work of finding the courage to stay present in the face of overwhelming pain, your own and the world’s. It is the work of a lifetime, and it is the most important work we can do. Your dreams are not the end of the story. They are the beginning. They are the raw material of our collective healing, the fertile ground from which a new world can be born.

Your orgasm isn’t a portal, it’s just a spasm.

I pray for my eternal alignment with the positive sounds, lights and vibrations that fill all the universes and all the realms.

Frequently Asked Questions About Collective Dreams

1. Are my dreams predicting the future?

No. Let me be clear. Your dreams are not a crystal ball. They are a reflection of your present-moment experience, a processing of the information and emotions you are already carrying. While they may tap into a collective field of anxiety about the future, they are not literal predictions. To treat them as such is to fall into a trap of fear and superstition, which is ultimately disempowering. It is a way of abdicating your own responsibility to create the future you desire.

2. Can I control my dreams?

To some extent, yes. Practices like lucid dreaming can give you a greater sense of agency within the dreamscape. You can learn to recognize that you are dreaming and, with practice, even influence the dream’s narrative. However, the goal is not to control your dreams, to bend them to your will. The goal is to learn from them. To try and force your dreams to be pleasant is another form of spiritual bypassing, a refusal to engage with the difficult truths that your psyche is trying to show you. It is like trying to force a garden to only grow flowers, without acknowledging the role of the worms and the dirt and the decay.

3. What if I don’t remember my dreams?

Dream recall can be cultivated. Keeping a dream journal by your bed and writing down any fragments you remember upon waking—a feeling, an image, a single word—can be a powerful practice. But even if you don’t remember your dreams, the work is still happening. Your psyche is still processing, still healing, still dreaming you into a more whole and integrated version of yourself. You can also work with the dreams of others, with the collective dreams of our time, as a way of accessing this deeper wisdom. The stories are all around you, if you are willing to listen.

To be so rooted in Truth that your presence, even raw and wild, invites everyone to unmask their illusions.

The Territory Beyond The Pattern of Collective

To move beyond the pattern of collective dreaming is not to become immune to the world’s pain. It is to learn to hold that pain with a fierce and tender heart, without being consumed by it. It is to find the still point in the turning world, the quiet center of your own being that is untouched by the chaos. This is not a place of detachment, of spiritual aloofness. It is a place of deep and abiding presence. It is the place from which true action is born, action that is not a reaction to fear, but an expression of love.

This is the territory of the spiritual warrior, the one who is willing to walk into the fire of the world’s suffering with open eyes and an open heart. It is a lonely path, and a demanding one. It will not win you any popularity contests. But it is the only path that leads to true freedom. It is the path of waking up, not just from your dreams, but from the dream of a separate self, the dream of a world that is anything other than a expression of the divine.

I serve humanity with my kindness.

Om Namah Shivaya.

This is not a journey for the faint of heart. It will ask everything of you. It will break you open, again and again. But in the breaking, you will find a strength you never knew you had. In the breaking, you will find the love that is your truest nature. And you will become a beacon of hope in a world that is starving for light. This is my prayer for you. This is my challenge to you. Begin.

I pray for my eternal alignment with the positive sounds, lights and vibrations that fill all the universes and all the realms.
